categoryComments.comments: Best is surfing, but worst is the level inclusivity for LGBTQ.
Two good lectures, not enough contact time
The carpet

Very accessible resources, the libraries are brilliant spaces, safe-feeling, supportive staff 
Everything is so expensive, especially sports and gym memberships.
Very broad variety of optional modules, lots of choice within course

Sometimes issues are dealt with really quickly, sometimes not. An example of this is we had a HORRENDOUS fly infestation about a month into uni and it took a good week and a half for any action to be taken, despite apparently being a recurring issue.

Amazing facilities available to all students, especially to study and work in. The student union frequently runs activities for all sorts of interests and the life as a student in the actually city is great. there is quite a few places to explore around Exeter itself, but in the city, there is a decent amount of places to spent time with friends or studying away from campus.
The campus is quite pretty too and despite it being on lots of hills, it's a nice place to be and walk around

The contact time is good, there is a lot of work that needs to be completed outside of contact hours for my course so im glad they afford me this time. 
The content is broad and you can focus in on lots of different specifics of historical content. However, one of the core modules seems a little convoluted and spread out for no reason.
 Study skills sessions offered is useful

Poor wifi connection often, this is normally resolved relatively quickly though.

The location is on the opposite side of campus from the city centre so this makes some things a bit more difficult.

The value for money compared to an accommodation in the SAME location is not as great, Moberly is much cheaper even though the only drawback is a shared bathroom with one person.
The halls are overall quite nice to live in
It's a great place to study because of the campus and the facilities that are available, such as the library and cafes.
I think there could be more contact time, but I like the blend of seminars and lectures.
The kitchen could be larger for 8 people sharing a space, but I love the location and modern appearance.

Exeter's campus has some very nice aspects - eg., the natural spaces, ponds, and so on. However, there is a lot of wastage and inefficiency in the name of technological progress. The campus is covered in TVs which just show one power point slide - those could have been posters. The gym's electronic barriers are constantly breaking, and more trouble than they're worth.

Some of the Student Societies here are great, but the Students Guild can be a very bureaucratic and cumbersome organisation.

Some Professors are incredibly engaging, whilst others are not. I believe the variation in teaching skills of my Professors is the most important factor - ie, how good they are at engaging students with their subjects. I believe that the underfunding of the HASS department significantly contributes towards this. Whilst the underfunding of HASS is an issue all over the world, it isn't underfunded at certain top universities who Exeter is supposedly trying to rival/model themselves on. 

 Even within the HASS department, I have seen the contrast between the more professional History department and the more scattered Ancient History department. I believe these two very similar subjects and departments should be more closely integrated. I have met a lot of other History and Ancient History students, and I think a greater collusion between the two would be highly beneficial.
Only catered accoms were eligible for the low income accom bursary, forcing me into choosing catered. I don't understand why this had to be a case, and the vegetarian options were very low quality.
I have no improvements, its perfect!
I find everything perfect.
The wifi is sometimes poor and I've heard of people having bad experiences with safety in Glasney.

There are plenty of study spaces which offer quiet zones for silent work on essays as well as areas for group work. The student guild make sure that student life outside of studying is of good quality as well as in uni. There are many support teams such as well-being which allow students to be supported and encouraged

I have a good amount of contact hours which allows questions and content to be covered effectively. Much of this content is covered in person which i believe is useful for understanding and concentration. I always feel as though i can contact my lecturers and seminar leaders for any concerns

The best aspect is how the lecturers are really nice. The facilites are great and so is the libary making my course much easier. To improve maybe make the lectures a little more interactive as that helps some people learn better
Good hours, lecturers are easy to contact and the content is taught well
The halls arent great, the carpets were dirty when i arrived, the fridges could be a bit bigger.  Reception is nice

reviews.courseDisplayName: Ancient History with Study Abroad - BA (Hons)

categoryComments.comments:
The teacher are friendly and helpful
It takes a while to walking over the strap hill to  the main campus( building (forum)

It good to have detailed information history overall understanding 
Lack of choice in elective courses
Student life is great so is the course societies are fun to join.
The lectures are interesting and are mostly recorded apart from one where you have to show up for.
It's convenient as it is close to the town centre and literally two doors away from Tesco.

The variety and quality of societies at exeter are very high  with many many trips and socials. One  thing I do not like is how (at least in history) is how there are different people and places for seemingly every lecture which in a total of 400 taking the course makes it harder to know the people you are taking the course with

The different modules are very good and very well taught. However some lecturers speak bad English which makes it hard to understand and  the lecturers (for me) have already been changed around a couple of times

The block I am staying in is the cheapest but I was very lucky to get on site non private accommodation in exeter this year as many did not and were forced to go private even though they had a confirmed offer. The block itself is pretty cramped, this makes it hard because out of 11 people the kitchen can only really have 2 to 3 cooking at a time. Furthermore there is no where near enough fridge space as I am currently using a single small shelf even though we have separated it pretty equally. The allocation was all male even though everybody picked mixed but that may have been because not an equal amount applied. The showers are good. The room walls are very soundproof which is very good.

Exeter’s approach to teaching- both from the dedication of staff to its wellbeing focus- is wonderful, especially when I have compared my experiences to friends at other universities. 

However, the general culture of students at Exeter is what is difficult for me. The ‘Exetah’ stereotype is relatively true and, as a working class boy from South Wales, this sometimes makes me feel unable to take part in social events or societies. Likewise; although not ran directly by the university, the costs associated with some societies- especially sports- are astronomical compared to similar societies at other major universities. 

The university itself is wonderful, being one of the greenest campus’s I have ever seen, but is sadly placed at the top of a very large hill far away from most cheap accommodation. Perhaps pointing out affordability, some university amenities- namely the Ram Bar- are very affordable. However, some simply are not- Exeter university is the only institution I know of who have chosen to have a Pret A Manger over a Greggs on campus. For most students, this is unaffordable. 

I have loved my first year at Exeter and look forward to the next two, but I do sometimes feel that I do not belong here, do not have enough money to take part in everything I want, etc.

Exeter takes pride in the fact it makes you read a lot to understand your course. Beyond one or two lecturers who read off of PowerPoints, they are each very knowledgeable and very good at explaining the intricacies of their topics. Exeter has some wonderful field experts for history too!

Lafrowda, at about £202/week for a single bed en-suite was lovely but very unaffordable. I had friends staying in the centre of Cardiff for roughly half of that and receiving a room twice as big. The staff, security, and campus feel of Lafrowda was amazing, but the price (as well as that of all local shops) felt extortionate.
Great facilities to study outside and inside and beautiful grounds. Student union is lackluster
Classes themselves are fine as are facilities but contact hours for this course are abysmal, maybe 3 hours of actual contact per week

Exeter is a very safe place to live and small enough that public transport can take you within 5 to 10 minutes of anywhere you want to go, in terms of full time employment here after university it is pretty much non-existent and finding work whilst at school in a job market with maybe 2000 part time places and 30,000 students is extremely difficult

Value for money is standard, not overpriced or underpriced, facilities are good and maintenance is fast but the location is relatively far away and significantly below campus meaning you need to trek up a large hill to get to class

Green space is one of the best parts about the uni. really lovely campus, and even though they're always full the study spaces are nice. Libraries too, lots of single or group study spaces. the ones with soft chairs are my favourites. Campus uni so its all grouped together which means you get a really nice sense of community with seeing people you know all over the place. Oly 20 minutes from town.

Main complaint would be that the on-campus food is overpriced but I think that's standard for unis. The standard of first year halls seems to really depend on where you go and who you're with but they're all pretty nice and I had a great time with it in first year- there's a big range depending on what kind of experience you want, i was in Lafrowda and it was great, proximity to the campus was the best part.

The Guild does do a lot of activities which is cool so its always busy on campus and there's something going on. Buildings are all heated nicely. lemon grove wasn't open in my first year but is very close by.

The societies are all great so can highly recommend that. even with the tiny ones you go and there's usually a sense of community. i think that's the best thing about exeter overall.

Lecturers definitely the best part. It will really depend on what specific course and what specific modules you do, but I like that, since you can get very niche for each year of study, as well as the modularity which a lot of people do for languages. There's a criminology course really popular among the people I know doing STEM.  

In terms of history the first year is the worst- and it's still good. There's just significantly less specialisation, where you only do two optional modules and then three general ones, which makes sense for building a foundation. There's a lot of general lectures in first year which changes when you go into second year. It's mainly seminars and small two hour module lectures which is nice because you really get more in depth to whatever particular subject, particularly if the professor is really passionate about it, which they generally are. You generally get to know them a lot more than in first year and they're very involved in how the course goes/ getting feedback/ telling you how it works etc which is definitely helpful.

 It's about four contact hours a week for each module in second year and same in third year apart from the dissertation and dissertation prep. You also have optional contact hours to discuss essays etc which it is definitely worth using. There's a broad range of options to select from when you're choosing your modules. It's almost all in-person lectures apart from occasional lectures on technical aspects of the degree, or if there's a weather issue/ personal issue/ something like that. you can access most lectures on ELE if you miss them, just not seminars.

The main bulk of the degree is reading and the online access to exeter's library is what i will miss most from the course, they have so many online academic articles you can access.

Good value for money, had all the facilities, and someone would come in once a week to clean the kitchen provided it had been kept tidy. Generally exactly the same bedrooms as Birks, single beds and new room, all nice. Was well lit and didnt get too hot or cold depending on weather.

Great location. My longest walk to any lecture was 10 minutes and my shortest was 2. Has general uni accomodation rules, no fairylights etc, but they let you have silly things in the windows which is fun. 

They had a fire drill every so often and tested the alarm once a week on wednesdays at 10 i think? so you knew when it would be coming but wasn't ideal if you had slept in. But that's the same for any uni hall that has good fire safety

Kitchen was nice, they had kettle included i think? Nice and bright, two sinks, two fridges, and it was quite social since there was enough room for most of us in there (maybe not all cooking at the same time). The view from the window was really nice, too, you could see right over exeter and to all the trees. There were two different places for washing machines, and if you went to the ones next to the market place shop, there were usually free machines, though the ones y reception were almost always in use.

Friendly people, great campus, interesting lectures and lecturers.
Could be improved with cheaper/better access to the sports facilities and teams.
Lecturers are al very nice. It's all a good amount of time every week. Contact over the entire course is, as a whole, good.
Catered food is pretty good as a whole. The rooms are great and spacious. Its a nice community in the halls.
Decent
Decent
Good
Good

reviews.courseDisplayName: Ancient History and Archaeology with Employment Experience BA (Hons)

categoryComments.comments:
Pros:
- University has great resources. It has a three story library and access to original primary source material.
- Disability and ILP team are lovely and accomodating.
- Beautiful location. Exeter is a very historical city with plenty of opportunities to enjoy nature. Train station 20 minutes from campus which has routes across the country.
- Guild supports students through cost of living campaigns and feed your flat meal nights where they provide ingredients. They also provide free period products.
- Give it a go run by the guild is fun and enjoyable. You get to do things for free including pumpkin carving, glass painting etc.
- Campus £2 meal deal great cheaper alternative to other campus food.
- Campus is relatively clean.

Cons:
- Online library system is difficult to use and often does not give you the results you're looking for.
- Not enough study spaces on campus, meaning you're always fighting for a seat if you're planning on staying to do work.
- Campus is on a steep hill, which you have to climb pretty much every single day.
- Student union doesn't really do much and is not very good with supporting its societies.
- Not much nightlife so places are crowded and tickets sell out fast.
- Buying anything on campus is expensive
- There are hardly any benches on campus.
- Bus routes to campus are limited. There is only one bus that comes to campus and it only covers a very small percentage of the city.

Course content: 
- Kind of all over the place. The course is being changed, leading to us being the first year to try certain things out.
- Not all essay assessments. Had to make an academic poster for second year that was 40% of one module. I also had to write an article in the style of a history today magazine. This year, I am expected to do a podcast pitch.
- Lack of clarity in expectations and poor communication. One time a lecturer did not turn up for our lecture.
- Mostly taught in person, but contact hours are few. Lots of independent work.
- I have had staff voice to me that they do not like the course either.

Teaching:
- Teaching is very hit or miss. Some lectures are fantastic, whilst others are quiet, mumble or talk with zero enthusiasm for their subject, which is sad.
- Seminars can be difficult. I have sat through a few dominated by silence. Some feel a little pointless.
- A few seminar tutors have made me feel anxious and uncomfortable, which meant I did not want to engage or attend. However, most seminar tutors are lovely and enthused with their work.

Subject Facilities:
- My lessons are all over the place. I have zero this year in the designated humanities block (amory).
- Good access to primary materials for research, though it does take some digging to find what you're looking for.
- Digital Humanities Lab for digital research
- ELE pages often provide lots of further reading and access to texts. This is down to lecturers and seminar tutors.

I stayed in university halls for my first two years. My experiences were vastly different between halls, so I will talk about them separately. These halls cost the same amount of money.

Christow was a wonderful place to live. I felt safe and secure, using an electronic key to get into my flat and room. All room doors locked from the inside. It was close to campus. It was, however, not wheelchair friendly as it had steps down to the front door. These would sometimes become slippery, and I did fall over once or twice. They did not grit them in the winter which made them unsafe.

I was allocated a room on the second floor. It was a flat of 6 people, sharing a kitchen. I had a double bed and an ensuite bathroom. The cupboards in the kitchen were spacious and there was plenty of room for 6 people. We had a huge corner sofa in a separated lounge area. We had a kitchen table with 6 chairs, two fridges and freezers, one microwave, one oven, one kettle and 2 sets of 4 hobs. It felt homely and welcoming. Glide WiFi was not fantastic, but it worked.

We had four big recycling boxes, which meant that we did not have to take the recycling out as often.

Maintence were, in most cases, quick to respond and were very friendly. Our boiler broke at the end of the year, which they dealt with immediately. They also replaced our hobs after an electrical fault. My flat was lovely, kind and welcoming. We got along very well.

I did not enjoy living in Lafrowda MB. The front door opened with electronic key, but often stayed open for a while where anyone could follow you inside. You used an electronic key to get into my flat and room. All room doors locked from the inside. It was close to campus. It was not disability friendly.

Despite having a personal evacuation plan for fires, I was put on the top floor of the flat, which was four flights of stairs up. It was horrible walking up them every day as there was no lift. Moving in was a nightmare.

I was in a flat of 8 people, sharing a kitchen. I had a single bed and an ensuite wet room. Bedroom had a huge wrap around desk, but Bathroom had zero storage. 

The cupboards in the kitchen were tiny and could not even fit a dinner plate. It was definitely not big enough for 8 people. We had a kitchen table with 8 chairs, but it was not built for 8 chairs. We had two fridges and freezers, two microwaves, two ovens, two kettles, two sinks and 2 sets of 4 hobs. There was a tiny carpeted square with two sofas and a table at the side of the kitchen. 

Whilst Christow had felt like a home, this flat did not. My flatmates and I did not get on, and there were constant arguments about bins and recycling. Our cleaner refused to clean our flat as the bins were so full. 

Glide was even worse from this building as I was so high up. It stopped working twice throughout the year.  Maintenence were quick to respond again and lovely. 

Overall, if I was doing this review just off St Germans I would give it 5 stars. But my experience in Lafrowda was unpleasant. It is all a luck of the draw.

Exeter is a lovely city - student life is great if you enjoy the outdoors, sport, pubs, local culture, and the limited clubs we have here. It takes a bit of getting used to if you are from a large city. 

The Students' Guild has for a long time been a joke institution that has not only failed to reflect the concerns of said students but has actively worked against them. Fortunately, it seems to have improved a little this year and has introduced things like Money Week. Improving on last year's Guild is probably not such a big achievement, though, considering that they did literally nothing all-year except offer a couple of equivocal and vapid statements on the UCU strikes.

Ancient History is a enjoyable, engaging, and challenging course. The Classics department is, on the whole, extremely hardworking, supportive, friendly, and helpful. Unfortunately, the course does not offer any internships, but does help you develop important skills and experiences for the workplace. 

Tutor contact time has been minimal/non-existent for most of my time at Exeter.

Excellent safety and property management. We even had a support person from the University come round to ask how we were doing each week. Campus estate patrol were very vigilant and we felt quite safe. 

The location is ok. It's right next to the train station, but is off campus and therefore a bit socially isolated. 

The value for money is pretty poor. The accommodation was ok, but is fairly overpriced considering that it is off-campus and self-catered.

reviews.courseDisplayName: Ancient History with Study Abroad BA (Hons)

categoryComments.comments: Great campus if you are looking for that sort of thing, footpaths and ponds everywhere with benches which are really nice when it's sunny weather, great connections to the south west (dartmoor, exmouth, etc.), downside is if you like clubbing the night life is very subpar, I do not so it's fine but that is something to consider.

Almost all in person teaching rather than online, contact  hours are about 9-11 hours per week, weighted towards seminars rather than lectures. Seminars are really useful however I think often times I don't gain much from lectures as they are only an hour and are surface level, never actually used lecture content for an assignment.

I got allocated ground floor room next to the kitchen so I get a lot of people knocking on my window or have late conversations next to the smoke bin right outside my window, but that's just a short straw I guess. Really close to lessons, maximum 10m walk, also about 7m from the coop and 15m into town. Any complaints are usually solved on the day / day after, only other complaint is that the showers are wet rooms and the drain gets clogged a lot meaning the floor can flood easily.

The student life is very good with lots of opportunities and activities. What could be improved are the quality of the facilities in place to help with things like essays. I feel like sometimes the support is very repetitive and not specific enough.

I really enjoy the lectures and seminars and find them very helpful and engaging. What could be improved is the help with internships more specific to my course, history, which can be quite limiting given the overwhelming amount of internships for courses such as economics.
The location was ideal as I was not too far from campus and not too far from the city centre. The facilities in the halls were great, especially since mine offered en-suites.

The library is open 24/7 which is really nice and there are loads of place to do work so there is always variety however the cafes/shops all close at either 3pm or 8pm and if youre late night studying youre limited to the expensive prices in the vending machines if you want a snack or refreshments.

The lectures sometimes assume that you know certain things and had background knowledge in classics for example so they tend to skip through some content. However, the fact that the lectures are recorded means that if im confused about aspects then i can always cycle through the lectures or contact the lecturer pretty easily

You arent given a toaster,we had to buy that ourselves, the kettle leakes everywhere when you pour from it, the microwave broke really easily and within the first few weeks of being there anf the shower door itself has come off of the hinges and no one seems to want to fix it. 
The walk to uni itself is about 20 minutes all up hill so you really have to like walking and have to use a lot of effort when going to uni each day.

reviews.courseDisplayName: Ancient History and Archaeology with Employment Experience Abroad BA (Hons)

categoryComments.comments:
The university stuff are fantastic in my experience, the help and support is always there. However, assessment feedback usually takes a very long time to get back.

Uni facilities are also very good, the library is fantastic though it could be a bit bigger as it can get very busy.

But exeter has a fantastic mix of city and country living whilst also having the sea side 20 mis away

Tutor contact time is there if you need it however, personally i don’t talk to my tutors that often but they are fantastically helpful when i do.

In terms of work prospects and internships, that is something i have neither looked into or have been told about
My halls were quite expensive. Around 170 a week for a single room with en-suite. But the on campus security would patrol often and come and fix things when needed

The campus is beautiful and easy to access from the town, but it is very far from the rest of the country.  There are many places to study and the library is well-stocked, especially since you can request books from other universities. 
They do say that missing a certain number of classes will prompt a check via email, but I've never actually seen this happen, even when people do.

There are no history or humanities specific areas within the university, so students will always be mixing with other courses, could be good or bad depending on your attitude. 
Seminars are held once a week for each module, meaning that you have many opportunities to meet with the tutors, and office hours are easy to find if they don't tell you themselves or attach it to the bottom of their emails.

There is no real way of determining that you end up with similar people, even if you ask for a quiet flat it is likely that you will still be surrounded by louder flats. Security are easy to contact and will attend issues relatively quickly.

Exeter is the perfect student city, especially for people living away from home for the first time. Everything is within a convenient walking distance and there is only really one main high street, so unlike studying in London for instance, you won't be overwhelmed. The Streatham campus is like a completely different district of the city, up on a hill, but only twenty minutes walk from the city centre. Once you get there, you feel instantly safe and relaxed as the campus has a very welcoming atmosphere. There is also a real sense of people working hard here, a very professional environment. The Forum is the most impressive building, with a 24/7 library and a selection of handy shops- a Marketplace and a Pret A Manger. The Starbucks on the second floor is a different matter, it seems very strange to put such an expensive shop on campus- it doesn't really encourage sensible spending. There are a wide range of fantastic facilities across the campus, including many different study areas, so if one seems very busy you can always go to a different one. The University is home to many unique societies that really make the Exeter student experience. The best part of the campus is the wildlife and the trees everywhere. In my first year, I will always remember seeing two deer by the pond near the Laver building and a red fox near East Park. There is also a campus cat, Napoleon, but that doesn't stop other cats from exploring the area, along with many dogs out for walks. All things considered, the University of Exeter and especially the Streatham campus is a wonderful place to study.

The course is largely taught in person, with the exception of one module in the second year which has had a number of online workshops. The lecturers are generally fantastic and bring a wide range of different areas of expertise to their lectures, meaning that each time you go to one, you always get a new perspective. The first year has plenty of contact hours, while the second year gives you less, but in doing so gives you more time to do your own research. Students have the freedom to book office hours with their tutors in which they can discuss any concerns or get advice on work personally tailored to them. The best part of the History BA course is the shrinking number of assessments, as the department is really trying to reduce the pressure on students. This has made the course much more enjoyable as it gives us the opportunity to really absorb ourselves in the assessments we do have, and do much better at them. There is also a wide range of modules to choose from each year. One curious thing about this course, and seemingly most university courses, that new students often aren't aware of is the fact that in most cases you won't have any lessons in the Summer Term. This is the assessment term when students are left to their own devices to complete their end of year assignments. It might have been helpful to have been told about this before joining the course, for clarity, although this seems to be a problem with most universities and definitely not just Exeter.

This accommodation is very modern and a popular choice with history students. Strangely, most of my course-mates seemed to also be living there in the first year. The facilities are excellent and the room size is just right. The worst part was probably the fire alarms which went off ridiculously often. Perhaps better guidance should have been given around cooking safety. An annoying feature of the accommodation is the cooking hobs which time out after five minutes, meaning that you have to keep turning them on all the time. This can be very frustrating when cooking a meal, only to find that you haven't actually been cooking anything for the last fifteen minutes.

Very beautiful campus with lots of lovely trees and green spaces. Mostly nice buildings. Good library. Some good professors. Pretty good societies and sports clubs.

However, let down by very left wing/woke poltitical agenda, which has affected teaching and is generally found everywhere I look. Almost all emails I recieve from the uni are about some woke issue, such as BLM LGBT, climate etc, which is frankly exceedingly boring, let alone if you don't agree with it. My professor once insinuated that Cleopatra was black, despite there being not a shred of evidence. I have found entire lectures being used as propaganda for some woke issue. The climate produced by such dogmatic and domineering views and people means actual debate is silenced. I know personally professors who despise all this rubbish, but are afraid of speaking out because of the group-think, herd mentality and intolerance of opinions. 

I have also fond many professors, at least in my subject as teaching like primary school teachers, which i find quite insulting.

Lastly I have generally found the people on my course have very little genuine inteterest and are simply doing it because, hey anicent history or archaeology sounds fun. This means there are a lot of people who frankly shouldnt be there. It is very rare I find people who are actually passionate or knowledgable about their subject. This defintely affects discourese in lectures and seminars, and i have often found myself as the only one talking.

The access to internships scheme was good.

Teaching quality is very varied. I would say perhaps 20% of my professors have been any good.

I have found my tutors know absolutely nothing about how well a student is doing academically. Very varied how often they reach out to you.
Duryard is a lovely quite green place. Really loved it there, just got rubbish flatmates however.

Having incredibly helpful Library Correspondents and amazing library resources, as well as proper study spaces on campus has been amazing in my third year. 

Although the independence of University work can be quite challenging and can knock confidence. Having examples of how to structure and improve your writing in classes would be greatly beneficial.

The best aspect of the course is varied topics 
A challenge aspect is difficulty with understanding how to improve
Close location to campus was incredibly handy and the modern features was perfect for creating a home away from home
X

The university campus is gorgeous and has plenty of space to explore during the nice weather. There are also an abundance of seating areas indoors including access to study spaces. The campus accommodations are well maintained and looked after as well as in a good location close to campus. The campus has a sports bar and lots of places to get food such as the amata cafe and koofi. The location is great with transport services into Falmouth for the beach and nightlife as well as Truro.

The university has a large selection of modules to choose from such as European history, people’s history, public history and world history. The module leaders craft the courses in a way that most of the study is independent with weekly lectures and seminars. There is a large archive open to students for research as well as both a physical and online library

Due to location and being a tourist destination, the area isn’t the cheapest but there are lots of opportunities for work from small independent businesses to large corporations such as Asda Sainsbury’s Lidl McDonalds and Wetherspoons.

Halls are expensive but are right on campus each room has its own en-suite bathroom and each flat is shared by a maximum of 8 people. The kitchen has two fridges and a freezer as well as stove oven microwave kettle and toaster. There is a tv in the kitchen as well. Rooms are spacious and each come with a large desk to work at

It has been lackluster. I have received hardly any contact hours and barely any support with my work. Covid has been a difficult time for everyone, however the uni has not tried to better the student experience at all, so that’s £9000 down the drain. I feel drained and alone, without the support that I’d need to perform my best.

categoryComments.questionTitle: Do you think your time at university this year has been value for money?

categoryComments.comments:
My department has a large amount of excellent staff who I've enjoyed working with. However, the university's study facilities are absolutely shocking for a university like Exeter; there is a total lack of study space, the library is tiny and poorly stocked, the computers are old and slow and even the chairs are horrible to sit on.
The university is not a welcoming place to those who aren't interested in partying and clubbing, or refuse to participate in 'lad culture'... Or are from a lower class background.

The university, for me, is the most enjoyable part. The refurbished parts on campus add to the university experience being that little bit better. The people on my course are great and the sport aspect is very enjoyable. Being able to push yourself more than ever. The lecturers are also passionate about what they teach. But the nightlife is quite an early night and has the potential to get boring here.

If you like the idea of a uni which is overcrowded, terrible night life, long queues for clubs, 95% rah populated and generally boring then this is the place for you. This was not for me so I withdrew. Otherwise the courses are good its educational and sports facilities are good and the accommodation is good.
